elements.sent-2411-001: Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs) provide a concise and expressive representation of dynamic scenes by modelling objects and their evolving relationships over time.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, real-world visual relationships often exhibit a long-tailed distribution, causing existing methods for tasks like Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) to produce biased scene graphs.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we propose ImparTail, a novel training framework that leverages curriculum learning and loss masking to mitigate bias in the generation and anticipation of spatio-temporal scene graphs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our approach gradually decreases the dominance of the head relationship classes during training and focuses more on tail classes, leading to more balanced training.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we introduce two new tasks—Robust Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation—designed to evaluate the robustness of STSG models against distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Extensive experiments on the Action Genome dataset demonstrate that our framework significantly enhances the unbiased performance and robustness of STSG models compared to existing methods.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.32]{paper_sec/images/TaskPicture.pdf} \caption{\textbf{(a) Long Tailed Distribution.} Predicates in Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph (STSG) datasets exhibit a long-tailed distribution; one such example is the Action Genome \cite{Ji_2019} dataset, whose distribution is described at the top right. \textbf{(b) Tasks.} We focus on two STSG tasks, including Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) on the left and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) on the right. VidSGG entails the identification of fine-grained relationships between the objects observed in the video, such as (Person, looking\_at, Paper Notebook) and (Person, not\_looking\_at, Paper Notebook) in respective frames to the left. \textbf{(c) Conventional Learning.} Due to the inherent long-tailed distribution of these datasets, models learnt using the conventional approaches focus more on the head classes and perform poorly on the tail classes as illustrated using the prediction scores of STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021} on contacting and attention relationships (refer middle row). \textbf{(d) Unbiased Learning.} To alleviate the dominance of head classes during training, in unbiased learning, we focus more on the tail classes, ensuring that the learnt models exhibit significantly better performance in predicting both head and tail classes (refer bottom row).} \label{fig:task_picture}
elements.sent-2411-007: As AI systems evolve to interact with complex, real-world environments, accurate, adaptive modeling becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, fields such as autonomous driving \cite{kim_et_al_3dsgg_2019 , human-robot interaction \cite{Migimatsu_2021 , and security surveillance \cite{yang2023track require predictive understanding that goes beyond conventional computer vision.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs) represent a powerful approach that can address this challenge; at a high level, STSGs are structured graphs where nodes correspond to objects and edges capture the evolving relationships between them over time \cite{Ji_2019, kim_et_al_3dsgg_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Unlike static scene graphs, STSGs offer nuanced video understanding, enabling autonomous systems to interpret interactions in real-time, anticipate changes, and make informed decisions~\cite{Song_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Yet, constructing effective STSGs that mirror the intricacies of the dynamic, ever-changing real world remains an unsolved problem.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, real-world relationships follow a highly imbalanced, long-tailed distribution, with a handful of head classes appearing frequently \cite{wang2020longtailedrb, cao_et_al_label_dist_margin_nips_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In contrast, the vast majority of so-called tail classes are rare but essential for a detailed understanding.
elements.sent-2411-014: This imbalance often skews the learned models towards common relationships, yielding an incomplete, incorrect, unsafe, and distorted interpretation of the scene \cite{tan_et_al_eql_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Addressing this challenge is vital: without a nuanced, detailed, and balanced representation, AI systems risk overlooking the subtle yet critical interpretations that define complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-016: To tackle this challenge, we introduce \methodnamenosp, an innovative framework designed to overcome the pervasive long-tail distribution bias in STSG generation.
elements.sent-2411-017: Via a novel curriculum learning strategy \cite{hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019 , ImparTail shifts attention from common head classes to tail classes, promoting a balanced representation that accurately captures all relationship types, not just the dominant ones.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, ImparTail employs loss masking techniques \cite{lin_et_al_iccv_focal_loss_2017, cao_et_al_label_dist_margin_nips_2019 that amplify the influence of rare classes during training, ensuring a more equitable learning process.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This controlled shift towards tail classes allows models to address data distribution disparities without compromising accuracy on head classes.
elements.sent-2411-020: Real-world data poses an additional challenge beyond bias: distributional shifts.
elements.sent-2411-021: Factors such as lighting, occlusions, and background changes often degrade model performance, affecting the reliability of scene graphs in real-world applications \cite{zhou_et_al_bbn_2019, shrivastava_et_al_hard_example_mining_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Conventional models struggle to generalize across these shifts, limiting their effectiveness in unpredictable conditions.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this, we introduce two new evaluation tasks\textemdash Robust Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation\textemdash that simulate realistic distributional shifts to assess model resilience under deployment-like conditions.
elements.sent-2411-024: We rigorously assess the performance of \methodname on the Action Genome dataset \cite{Ji_2019 , a key benchmark for video understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our results show that \methodname achieves high mean recall scores.
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, \methodname exhibits robust performance amid distributional shifts, demonstrating that it is well-suited for building robust real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, this paper makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-028: We introduce a novel, effective, unbiased learning framework for generating and anticipating Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs by leveraging Partial Stochastic Gradient Descent with Curriculum Learning \cite{hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019, matiisen_et_al_teacher_student_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our method is especially suited to address the challenges posed by the long-tailed distribution of real-world relationships.
elements.sent-2411-030: To assess the resilience of our model against real-world challenges, we systematically induce various corruptions in the input data \cite{tan_et_al_eql_2020, kang_et_al_iclr_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: We thoroughly estimate the robustness and generalization capabilities by evaluating the model's performance during the inference phase under these distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-032: We conduct extensive experiments on the Action Genome \cite{Ji_2019 benchmark dataset and compare it with existing state-of-the-art methods to empirically validate the efficacy of the proposed approach.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Image Scene Graph Generation (ImgSGG) focuses on representing static visual data—such as 2D and 3D images—as spatial graphs where objects are nodes and their relationships are edges.
elements.sent-2411-034: The field gained significant traction with the foundational Visual Genome project \cite{krishna_et_al_visual_2017 , which advanced research in 2D ImgSGG.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Building upon this work, Kim et al. \cite{kim_et_al_3dsgg_2019 extended the task to static 3D scene data by incorporating both RGB and depth information.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Object interactions over time provide richer contextual information for dynamic visual content like videos.
elements.sent-2411-037: Transforming this content into structured Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs)—with nodes representing objects and edges capturing temporal relationships—is known as Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG).
elements.sent-2411-038: Research in VidSGG has concentrated on improving representation learning through advanced object-centric architectures such as STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021 and RelFormer \cite{shit_et_al_relformer_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Shifting the focus from the identification and generation of scene graphs, Peddi et al. \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 recently introduced the Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) task, which aims to predict STSGs for future frames.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Alongside these developments, foundation models have propelled advancements in various ImgSGG task variants \cite{chen_et_al_expanding_scene_graph_2023, kim_et_al_llm4sgg_2023, zhou_et_al_vlprompt_2023, zhao_et_al_less_2023, li2023zeroshotvisualrelationdetection .. Unbiased Learning.}
elements.sent-2411-041: TEMPURA \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 and FlCoDe\cite{ khandelwal_correlation_2023 address the challenges posed by long-tailed datasets, such as those found in Action Genome \cite{Ji_2019 and VidVRD \cite{xindi_et_al_vid_vrd_2017 and propose methods for unbiased VidSGG.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Specifically, FloCoDe \cite{khandelwal_correlation_2023 mitigates bias by emphasizing temporal consistency and correcting the imbalanced distribution of visual relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Similarly, TEMPURA \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 addresses biases in relationship prediction with memory-guided training to generate balanced relationship representations and applies a Gaussian Mixture Model to reduce predictive uncertainty.. Note.}
elements.sent-2411-044: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to systematically investigate model biases in the SGA task and assess the robustness of both VidSGG and SGA models.. Long-Tail Learning.
elements.sent-2411-045: Long-tailed distributions with a few dominant classes (head classes) often overshadow a more significant number of underrepresented ones (tail classes).
elements.sent-2411-046: This class imbalance typically results in models that perform well on head classes but struggle to generalize to tail classes.
elements.sent-2411-047: To mitigate them, the research community has made significant strides in four directions, which include (a) Cost-Sensitive Learning \cite{huang_et_al_2016, wang_et_al_tail_2017, cui_et_al_2019, ridnik_et_al_asl_loss_2020, wu_et_al_distribution_balanced_2020, ouyang_et_al_factors_finetuning_2016, wang_et_al_tail_2017, you_et_al_exemplar_clustering_2018, zhang_et_al_range_loss_2017 , (b) Mixtures-Of-Experts \cite{Xiang2020LearningFM , (c) Resampling Techniques \cite{chawla_et_al_smote_2002, drummond_et_al_c45_kdd_2003, zhang_et_al_2021 , and (d) Specialized Architectures \cite{zhang_et_al_2021, zhou_et_al_bbn_2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Curriculum Learning (CL) is a training methodology that structures training by presenting simpler examples first and progressively introducing more complex ones.
elements.sent-2411-049: This approach aims to enhance learning efficiency by aligning the difficulty of training data with the model's learning capacity at each stage \cite{kumar_et_al_self_paced_2010, graves_et_al_auto_cr_2017, hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019, han_et_al_dyn_een_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Despite its potential benefits, implementing CL presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-051: A primary obstacle is the non-trivial task of distinguishing between easy and hard training samples.
elements.sent-2411-052: This differentiation often requires additional mechanisms, such as auxiliary neural networks acting as teachers or specialized algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-053: Difficulty measures can be predefined based on certain heuristics \cite{ionescu_et_al_diff_vis_search_2016 or learned automatically during the training process \cite{kumar_et_al_self_paced_2010, jiang_et_al_easy_samples_2014, weinshall_et_al_cr_2018, jiang_et_al_mentornet_2017, ren_et_al_ltrw_2018, hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019, matiisen_et_al_teacher_student_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Alongside difficulty assessment, a scheduling strategy is essential to determine when and how to introduce more challenging data \cite{graves_et_al_auto_cr_2017 .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.3]{paper_sec/images/Overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of \methodname \textbf{(a) Pipeline.} The forward pass of \methodname begins with an ORPU, where initial object proposals are generated for each observed frame. These object representations are then fed to STPUs designed to construct spatio-temporal context-aware relationship representations of interacting objects. \methodname applied to both tasks VidSGG and SGA remains mostly the same, with an additional LDPU unit added for SGA to anticipate relationship representations for future frames. These observed (for VidSGG)/ anticipated (for SGA) relationship representations are then decoded to construct STSGs. \textbf{(b) Conventional Loss.} Previous approaches estimated loss for all relationship predicates (head and tail classes). \textbf{(c) Masked Loss.} With the inherent long-tailed nature of the STSG datasets, conventional training results in biased VidSGG and SGA models. Thus, to de-bias the training and learn an unbiased model, in \methodname, instead of estimating loss for all relationship predicates, we estimate a masked loss, where we selectively mask the labels corresponding to dominant head classes and void their contribution in learning. \textbf{(d) Curriculum Learning.} In \methodname, we introduce a curriculum-based approach for masking relationship predicate labels during training. At each iteration, we adjust the selection of masked predicates to balance the class distribution progressively. As illustrated, initially, the model trains on the original, long-tailed distribution. As training advances, we systematically mask predicate labels from the head classes, gradually shifting the distribution toward uniformity. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-055: We borrow the notation from SceneSayer\cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 and briefly present it below.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $\mathcal{D}$ correspond to the ground truth annotations of an STSG dataset.
elements.sent-2411-057: Consider a video segment \( V_{1 ^{T \) from a video \( V \), modelled as a sequence of frames at discrete time steps \( t = 1, 2, \ldots, \Bar{T \): \begin{equation}V_{1}^{T} = \{ I^{1}, I^{2}, \ldots, I^{T} \}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Here, \( T \) denotes the total number of frames in the segment, which may vary between videos.
elements.sent-2411-059: In each frame \( I^{t \), we represent the scene using a scene graph that captures the visual objects and their pairwise relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let \( O^{t = \{ o_{1 ^{t , o_{2 ^{t , \ldots, o_{N(t) ^{t \} \) be the set of observed objects in frame \( I^{t \), where \( N(t) \) is the number of objects in that frame.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Each object \( o_{k ^{t \) is characterized by: A bounding box \( \mathbf{b _{k ^{t \in [0,1]^{4 \), representing its spatial location and A category label \( \mathbf{c _{k ^{t \in \mathcal{C \), where \( \mathcal{C \) is the set of all object categories.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: To describe relationships between objects, we define \( \mathcal{P \) as the set of all predicate classes representing possible spatio-temporal relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-063: For any pair of objects \( (o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t ) \), multiple predicates \( \{ p_{ijk ^{t \} \) may be associated, where each predicate \( p_{ijk ^{t \in \mathcal{P \).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: A relationship instance is then defined as the triplet: \begin{equation}r_{ijk}^{t} = (o_{i}^{t}, p_{ijk}^{t}, o_{j}^{t}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: Thus, the scene graph \( \mathcal{G ^{t \) for frame \( t \) consists of all such relationship triplets: \begin{equation}G^{t} = \{ r_{ijk}^{t} \}_{i,j,k}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For each observed object \( o_{i ^{t \) and each object pair \( (o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t ) \), we define probability distributions over object categories and predicate classes, respectively: \( \hat{\mathbf{c _{i ^{t \in [0,1]^{|\mathcal{C | \) is the probability distribution over object categories for \( o_{i ^{t \) and \( \hat{\mathbf{p _{ij ^{t \in [0,1]^{|\mathcal{P | \) is the probability distribution over predicate classes for the pair \( (o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t ) \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We distinguish and clarify the objectives of Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) as follows: (a) VidSGG.
elements.sent-2411-069: The primary goal of VidSGG is to construct a sequence of scene graphs \(\{\mathcal{G ^{t}\}_{t=1 ^{T}\) corresponding to the observed video segment \( V_{1 ^{T = \{ I^{t \}_{t=1 ^{T \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: This involves: Identifying objects \( \{ o_{k ^{t \}_{k=1 ^{N(t) \) within each frame \( I^{t \) and Determining all possible pairwise relationships \( \{ r_{ijk ^{t \}_{i,j,k \) among the detected objects.. (b) SGA.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The main objective of SGA is to generate scene graphs \( \{\mathcal{G ^{t}\}_{t=T+1 ^{T+H \) for future frames \( V_{T+1 ^{T+H = \{ I^{t \}_{t=T+1 ^{T+H \) of the video, using information from the observed segment \( V_{1 ^{T \).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Here, \( H \) denotes the anticipation horizon\footnote{Following the definition and assumptions from SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , we assume the continuity of observed objects in future frames---that is, \( \{ o_{i ^{t \}_{i=1 ^{N(t) = \{ o_{i ^{T \}_{i=1 ^{N(T) \) for all \( t > T \)---and focus on predicting the evolution of their relationships \( \{ \{ r_{ijk ^{t \}_{t=T+1 ^{T+H \}_{i,j,k \).. .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Building upon the literature in Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA), we implement three distinct strategies for constructing scene graphs:.
elements.sent-2411-074: With Constraint Strategy: This strategy imposes a unique interaction constraint between every pair of objects within a scene.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, for any two objects \((o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t )\), there exists exactly one relationship predicate \(p_{ij ^{t}\) (for each relationship category) that describes their interaction.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We include these relationship triplets \(\{ r_{ij ^{t \}_{ij}\) in the scene graph \(\mathcal{G ^{t}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: No Constraint Strategy: This approach allows for a more expressive graph structure by permitting multiple relationship predicates between any pair of interacting objects \((o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t )\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We incorporate all predicted relationship triplets \(\{ r_{ijk ^{t \}_{i,j,k}\) into the scene graph \(\mathcal{G ^{t}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Semi-Constraint Strategy: This strategy strikes a balance by permitting multiple relationships between object pairs \((o_{i ^{t , o_{j ^{t )\) only if their confidence scores exceed a predefined threshold.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Specifically, we add relationship triplets \(\{ r_{ijk ^{t \}_{i,j,k}\) to the scene graph \(\mathcal{G ^{t}\) when the confidence score satisfies \(\hat{p _{ijk ^{t > \theta_{k}\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We propose \methodnamenosp, a framework for unbiased Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA).
elements.sent-2411-082: In \methodnamenosp, we adopt an object-centric relationship representation processing pipeline as illustrated in Fig~\ref{fig:overview .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Thus, it can be easily extended to de-bias any VidSGG or SGA method built on an object-centric relationship representation processing pipeline\footnote{Most VidSGG \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021, Feng_2021, nag_et_al_tempura_2023, khandelwal_correlation_2023 and SGA \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 approaches share a similar object-centric relationship representation processing pipeline.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We further note that recent SOTA VidSGG methods still rely on the object-centric pipelines introduced by STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021 and DSGDetr \cite{Feng_2021 .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, this method allows the model to progressively balance the class distribution by concentrating on underrepresented classes.
elements.sent-2411-086: The following sections provide a comprehensive overview of the key components and training methodologies of \methodnamenosp.
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, In Sec.~\ref{sec:Pipeline , we outline a detailed explanation of ORPU and STPU, including their respective inputs and outputs within a single training iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-088: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Curriculum , we provide a detailed description of the proposed Curriculum-Guided Mask Generation Strategy, and in Sec.~\ref{sec:MaskedLoss , we present the final change imparted in the loss function for both VidSGG and SGA tasks.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{paper_sec/images/CorruptionsImage.pdf} \caption{\textbf{(a) Robustness Evaluation Pipeline:} We outline a methodology to evaluate the robustness of trained VidSGG and SGA models under input distribution shifts. Specifically, we introduce systematic corruptions into the frames of test videos, which are then fed as input to the trained models. \textbf{(b) Corrupted Frames:} We illustrate the resultant frames obtained by inducing various categories of corruptions.} \label{fig:input_corruptions}
elements.sent-2411-089: Pipeline Both ORPU and STPU are designed to be compatible with any VidSGG or SGA method.
elements.sent-2411-090: As most existing approaches use similar processing pipelines, we outline the essential architectural components required for our framework to effectively de-bias their methods\footnote{Current methods in the literature typically incorporate at least one additional component beyond those specified here.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Our experiments directly integrated the ORPU and STPU units from the selected methods .
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, we extract visual features ($\botsequence{\mathbf{v ^t {i {1 {N(t)}$), bounding boxes ($\botsequence{\mathbf{b ^t {i {1 {N(t)}$), and object category distributions ($\botsequence{\hat{\mathbf{c ^t {i {1 {N(t)}$), for object proposals $\botsequence{o^t {i {1 {N(t)}$ in the observed frames.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: We use a transformer encoder \cite{vaswani_et_al_2017 to integrate temporal information and generate consistent object representations.}
elements.sent-2411-094: It is given by: \begin{equation}\mathbf{V}_{i}^{(n)} = \operatorname{ObjectEncoder}\left(\mathbb{Q} = \mathbb{K} = \mathbb{V} = \mathbf{V}_{i}^{(n-1)} \right)\end{equation} (\RNum{2) Spatio-Temporal Context Processing Unit (STPU): To learn spatio-temporal context-aware relationship representations, we first construct representations for interacting objects, then refine spatial context with an encoder, and finally use a transformer encoder to integrate spatio-temporal context.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically, we define the relationship representation for each observed frame $I^{t}$ as $\mathbf{z _{ij ^t$, where $\mathbf{Z ^{t}$ is formed by stacking all relationship features $\{\mathbf{z _{ij ^{t}\}_{ij}$ corresponding to the objects within the frame.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: This stacked representation $\mathbf{Z ^{t}$ is then passed through the transformer encoder to produce spatial-context-aware representations.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Next, we construct the matrix $\mathbf{Z _{ij}$ by stacking the relationship representations $\topsequence{\mathbf{z _{ij {t {1 {T}$ over all observed frames.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: This matrix $\mathbf{Z _{ij}$ is then passed through an encoder, which aggregates temporal information across the stacked relationship representations: \begin{equation}{[Z^{t}}]^{(n)} = \operatorname{SpatialEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = [{Z^{t}}]^{(n-1)} \right) Z_{ij}^{(n)} = \operatorname{TemporalEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = Z_{ij}^{(n)} \right)\end{equation} (\RNum{3) Relationship Predicate Decoders: We utilize a two-layer multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to decode the output relationship representations from STPU.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}{[Z^{t}}]^{(n)} = \operatorname{SpatialEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = [{Z^{t}}]^{(n-1)} \right) Z_{ij}^{(n)} = \operatorname{TemporalEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = Z_{ij}^{(n)} \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-099: We note that for SGA task, following SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , we employ two relationship predicate decoders.}
elements.sent-2411-100: One for the observed relationship representations and the other for the anticipated representations.
elements.sent-2411-101: They can be described as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:PredicateClassification} \hat{p}^t_{ij} = \operatorname{PredClassifier}_{observed}\left(z^t_{ij} \right), \forall t \in [1, T] \hat{p}^t_{ij} = \operatorname{PredClassifier}_{anticipated}\left(z^t_{ij} \right), \forall t \in [T+1, \bar{T}]\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: Devising curriculum learning approaches for any problem necessitates precise, problem-specific definitions of three key components \cite{matiisen_et_al_teacher_student_2017 : (a) the ability to rank tasks from easy to hard, (b) the capability to determine mastery over a specific task, and (c) a procedure that periodically integrates easier tasks with more challenging ones.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In this vein, \methodname employs a simple yet significantly effective curriculum learning method to generate masks for relationship predicate labels during training VidSGG and SGA models.
elements.sent-2411-104: Banking on the transformers' prowess in sequential processing tasks, we design the curriculum-guided mask generation process for VidSGG and SGA tasks using simple heuristics as follows: (a) Ranking: We assume that more data associated with a particular relationship predicate label indicates a better ability to predict that class.
elements.sent-2411-105: Therefore, we rank the hardness of relationship predicate learning based on the quantity of data available for them.
elements.sent-2411-106: The above discussion yields Alg~\ref{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation , where, as described, in each iteration, we progressively increase the number of masks over dominant relationship predicate labels, voiding their contribution.}
elements.sent-2411-107: During training, at each epoch $e$, as described above, curriculum-guided $\operatorname{MaskGenerator}$ provides us with a set of masks $\mathcal{M ^{(e)}$ for all the video segments in the dataset $\mathcal{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Now, let $\mathcal{L _{p^t_{ij }$ represent any loss defined over a single relationship predicate.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Upon application of the mask, the resultant masked loss $\mathscr{L _{p^t_{ij }$ is given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RelMaskedLoss} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ij}} = \mathbf{m}^t_{ij} * \mathcal{L}_{p^t_{ij}} = \begin{cases} \mathcal{L}_{p^t_{ij}} \text{if } \mathbf{m}^t_{ij} = 1 0 \text{if } \mathbf{m}^t_{ij} = 0 \end{cases}\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RelMaskedLoss} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ij}} = m^t_{ij} * L_{p^t_{ij}} =  L_{p^t_{ij}} if  m^t_{ij} = 1 0 if  m^t_{ij} = 0 \end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \SetCustomAlgoRuledWidth{\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon { \KwIn{Epoch: $e$, Sampling ratio: $\mathcal{S}$, Dataset Annotations: $\mathcal{D}$} \KwOut{Ground Truth Annotation Masks: $\mathcal{M}$} Compute total annotations $N = \sum_{v,f} |\mathcal{D}[v][f]|$\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Determine Target Counts ***} $N_{\text{target}} = \operatorname{round}(N \times (1 - (e \times \mathcal{S})))$\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Curriculum-based sampling probabilities $Prob[rel]$ ***}\; \textcolor{blue}{*** For an equally weighted multinomial distribution *** }\; Set $Prob[rel] = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}|}$ \; Sample target counts $Tar[rel]$ from Multinomial distribution: $Tar[rel] \sim \operatorname{Multinomial}(N_{\text{target}}, Prob[rel])$ \; \textcolor{blue}{*** Randomly sample instances of relationship predicates in the dataset based on the target counts for each relationship predicate and construct the filtered dataset ***} \; Construct filtered dataset $\mathcal{F}$ \; \textcolor{blue}{*** Reconstruct Filtered Ground Truth Annotations ***} \; Initialize empty mask list $\mathcal{M}$\; \ForEach{Video $v$ in $\mathcal{G}$}{ Initialize video mask $\mathcal{M}_v$\; \ForEach{Frame $f$ in $v$}{ Initialize frame mask $\mathcal{M}_f$\; \ForEach{Object $o$ in $f$}{ Initialize object mask $\mathcal{M}_o$\; Original relations $\mathcal{R}_{o}$ from $\mathcal{D}$\; Filtered relations $\mathcal{F}_{o}$ from $\mathcal{F}_r$\; \ForEach{Relation $rel$ in $\mathcal{R}_{o}$}{ \eIf{$rel \in \mathcal{F}_{o}[v][f]$}{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 1$\; }{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 0$\; } } Add $\mathcal{M}_o$ to $\mathcal{M}_f$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_f$ to $\mathcal{M}_v$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_v$ to $\mathcal{M}$\; } } \caption{Mask Generation} \label{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation}
elements.sent-2411-110: Thus, the objective when \methodname is employed to SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 includes (1) Object Classification Loss, (2) Masked Predicate Classification Loss over observed relationship representations, (3) Masked Predicate Classification Loss over anticipated relationship representations, (4) Bounding Box Regression Loss and (5) Reconstruction Loss and is given in Eq~\ref{eq:SGA .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: To evaluate the robustness of VidSGG and SGA models against distribution shifts caused by input corruptions, we introduce two tasks: Robust Video Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation.
elements.sent-2411-112: Fig~\ref{fig:input_corruptions illustrates the evaluation pipeline established to assess the trained models under these distribution shifts induced by input corruptions.}
elements.sent-2411-113: As depicted in the figure, we employ a standard set of corruptions commonly used in adversarial robustness research.

elements.sent-2411-114: We apply \methodname to generate and anticipate STSGs on the Action Genome dataset~\cite{Ji_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: We prepare the dataset by following the pre-processing step described in \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021, peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: The dataset includes 35 object classes and 25 relationship classes.
elements.sent-2411-117: These 25 relationship classes are grouped into three categories, namely: (1) Attention Relations comprise relationship classes which primarily describe the attention of the subject towards the object, (2) Spatial Relations comprise relationship classes that describe the spatial relationship between the subject and objects, and (3) Contacting Relations comprises relationship classes that indicate different ways the subject contacts the object \footnote{Remark.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The dataset predominantly features videos with a single individual interacting with objects across diverse real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-119: Within the Action Genome framework, a subject-object pair can exhibit multiple types of spatial and contact relationships.. .
elements.sent-2411-120: We evaluate our models using the standard Recall@K and meanRecall@K metrics, where $K$ takes values within the set \{10, 20, 50\}.
elements.sent-2411-121: The Recall@K metric measures the model's capability to predict the relationships between observed objects in future frames.
elements.sent-2411-122: The long-tailed distribution of relationships in the training set \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 can generate biased scene graphs, causing frequently occurring relationships to dominate the Recall@K metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-123: To address this imbalance, we utilize the mean recall metric introduced in \cite{chen2019knowledgeembedded , a more balanced metric that scores the model's generalisation to all predictive classes.}
elements.sent-2411-124: In the following sections, we will assess the performance of \methodname on four tasks, namely: (a) VidSGG, (b) SGA, (c) RobustVidSGG and (d) RobustSGA.
elements.sent-2411-125: For each task, we first select a few methods, train and evaluate them as per the instructions, then adapt them to \methodname framework, re-train and evaluate them.
elements.sent-2411-126: For VidSGG and Robust VidSGG tasks, we selected STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021 and DSGDetr \cite{Feng_2021 as our baselines and for the SGA task, we selected STTran+ \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , STTran++ \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , DSGDetr+ \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , DSGDetr++ \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , SceneSayerODE \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , SceneSayerSDE \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 as our baselines.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We follow \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021 and use three different settings to evaluate models for the VidSGG task.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Each setting has a different level of information provided as input to the models.
elements.sent-2411-129: Similarly, for the SGA task, we follow SceneSayer~\cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 and use three different settings to evaluate models.}
elements.sent-2411-130: We began by training and evaluating the selected baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-131: Subsequently, we adapted these models to incorporate \methodnamenosp, followed by retraining and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-132: Our experiments encompassed three VidSGG modes SGDET, SGCLS, and PREDCLS and were reported under three distinct settings: (1) With Constraint, (2) No Constraint, and (3) Semi Constraint.
elements.sent-2411-133: As demonstrated in Tab~\ref{tab:sgg_combined_recall_results , models trained using \methodname consistently outperformed baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Insights: Our results indicate a significant improvement of approximately $\sim$12\% in mR@10 across all configurations for SGDET, a $\sim$22\% increase in mR@10 for the SGCLS mode, and at least $\sim$20\% improvement in mR@10 for the PREDCLS mode.
elements.sent-2411-135: Moreover, our results are on par with and occasionally surpass SOTA unbiased learning methods \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: Importantly, these significant improvements were achieved without introducing any new architectural components.
elements.sent-2411-137: To train and evaluate the SGA models, we adopted a methodology similar to that of VidSGG.
elements.sent-2411-138: Initially, we trained and assessed the selected baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-139: Subsequently, we integrated these models into the \methodname framework, followed by retraining and re-evaluating their performance.
elements.sent-2411-140: As demonstrated in Table~\ref{tab:sga_mean_recall_results , Models trained using \methodname consistently outperformed their baseline variants.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Insights: The results demonstrate a improvement of $\sim$12\% in mr@10 across all modes in STTran++ and DSGDetr++.
elements.sent-2411-142: Although the performance is sometimes inferior, it closely aligns with the original mean recall values.
elements.sent-2411-143: We also note that SceneSayer ODE/SDE models trained using \methodname demonstrated an improvement of $\sim$20\%.
elements.sent-2411-144: We evaluated VidSGG and SGA models trained with \methodname and those trained using traditional approaches across 16 input corruption scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-145: Our experiments revealed that introducing noise to the inputs generally led to a decline in performance.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, models trained with \methodname exhibited a less severe performance drop than baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-147: For example, in the PREDCLS mode under the No Constraint setting with Gaussian Noise for STTran, the baseline model’s mR@10 decreased from 45.7 to 31.4.
elements.sent-2411-148: In contrast, the \methodname-trained model only dropped from 65.5 to 57.5.
elements.sent-2411-149: To maintain brevity, only a subset of the results are reported in the main paper, and we defer additional results to the supplementary text.
elements.sent-2411-150: Insights: (1) We noticed that the drop in performance of both VidSGG and SGA models trained using \methodname is lower than the models trained with traditional approaches.
elements.sent-2411-151: In this paper, we tackled the pervasive challenge of long-tailed distribution bias in Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph (STSG) generation, often leading to models that overlook rare but critical relationships in dynamic scenes.
elements.sent-2411-152: We introduced \methodnamenosp, a framework that employs curriculum learning and loss masking techniques to shift the learning focus toward tail classes without sacrificing performance on head classes.
elements.sent-2411-153: To further evaluate the practical applicability of our model, we proposed two new tasks: Robust Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation.
elements.sent-2411-154: These tasks simulate real-world distributional shifts, allowing us to assess the robustness of STSG.
elements.sent-2411-155: Through extensive experiments on the Action Genome, \methodname demonstrated superior performance in mean recall scores and maintained robustness against various input corruptions.
elements.sent-2411-156: Future work includes developing robust learning techniques \cite{ilyas2019adversarialexamplesbugsfeatures, peddi_et_al_uai_2022, carlini2019evaluatingadversarialrobustness for both VidSGG and SGA tasks and exploring the application of unbiased VidSGG and SGA in long-tailed regimes such as error recognition \cite{peddi2024captaincook4ddatasetunderstandingerrors, sener2022assembly101largescalemultiviewvideo , action anticipation \cite{nawhal2022rethinkinglearningapproacheslongterm etc. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs) provide a concise and expressive representation of dynamic scenes by modeling objects and their evolving relationships over time.
elements.sent-2411-158: To this end, we propose \methodname, a novel training framework that leverages loss masking and curriculum learning to mitigate bias in the generation and anticipation of spatio-temporal scene graphs.
elements.sent-2411-159: Unlike prior methods that add extra architectural components to learn unbiased estimators, we propose an impartial training objective that reduces the dominance of head classes during learning and focuses on underrepresented tail relationships.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our curriculum-driven mask generation strategy further empowers the model to adaptively adjust its bias mitigation strategy over time, enabling more balanced and robust estimations.
elements.sent-2411-161: To thoroughly assess performance under various distribution shifts, we also introduce two new tasks—Robust Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation—offering a challenging benchmark for evaluating the resilience of STSG models.
elements.sent-2411-162: Extensive experiments on the Action Genome dataset demonstrate the superior unbiased performance and robustness of our method compared to existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-163: Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs) are structured graphs where nodes correspond to objects and edges capture the evolving relationships between them over time \cite{Ji_2019, kim_et_al_3dsgg_2019 .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{paper_sec/images/Motivation.pdf} \caption{Overview. Row-1: Existing training pipelines in the literature for VidSGG/SGA tasks. Row-2: Prior unbiased learning work, exemplified by the supplementary architectural modules and loss functions. Row-3 (\methodname): A framework through which any prior object-centric, representation learning–based VidSGG/SGA method can be adapted to learn corresponding unbiased estimator.} \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-164: To tackle this challenge, we introduce \methodnamenosp, a novel framework designed to overcome long-tail distribution bias in STSG generation.
elements.sent-2411-165: It employs a novel loss masking technique (similar in spirit to \cite{lin_et_al_iccv_focal_loss_2017, cao_et_al_label_dist_margin_nips_2019 ) that amplifies the influence of rare classes during training, ensuring an equitable learning process.}
elements.sent-2411-166: This controlled shift towards tail classes allows models to address data distribution disparities without significantly compromising performance on head classes.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[scale=0.32]{paper_sec/images/TaskPicture.pdf} \caption{\textbf{(a) Long Tailed Distribution.} Predicates in Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph (STSG) datasets exhibit a long-tailed distribution; one such example is the Action Genome \cite{Ji_2019} dataset, whose distribution is described at the top left. \textbf{(b) Tasks.} We focus on two STSG tasks, including Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) on the left and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) on the right. VidSGG entails the identification of fine-grained relationships between the objects observed in the video, such as (Person, looking\_at, Paper Notebook) and (Person, not\_looking\_at, Paper Notebook) in respective frames to the left. SGA aims to anticipate the evolution of these relationships to (Person, touching, Cup), and eventually, (Person, drinking\_from, Cup) \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024}. \textbf{(c) Conventional Learning.} Due to the inherent long-tailed distribution of these datasets, models learnt using the conventional approaches focus more on the head classes and perform poorly on the tail classes as illustrated using the prediction scores of STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021} on contacting and attention relationships (refer middle row). \textbf{(d) Unbiased Learning.} To alleviate the dominance of head classes during training, in unbiased learning, we focus more on the tail classes, ensuring that the learnt models exhibit significantly better performance in predicting both head and tail classes (refer bottom row).} \label{fig:task_picture}
elements.sent-2411-167: Thus, this paper makes the following contributions\footnote{Project: \href{https://rohithpeddi.github.io/\#/impartail {https://rohithpeddi.github.io/\#/impartail :.}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: We introduce a novel, effective, unbiased learning framework for generating and anticipating Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs by leveraging Curriculum-Guided Masked Training with Partial Gradients \cite{hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019, matiisen_et_al_teacher_student_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-169: The field gained significant traction with the foundational Visual Genome project \cite{krishna_et_al_visual_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: Shifting the focus from the identification and generation of scene graphs, Peddi et al. \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 recently introduced the Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) task, which aims to predict STSGs for future frames.. Unbiased Learning.}
elements.sent-2411-171: TEMPURA \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 and FlCoDe\cite{ khandelwal_correlation_2023 address the challenges posed by long-tailed datasets, such as those found in Action Genome \cite{Ji_2019 and VidVRD \cite{xindi_et_al_vid_vrd_2017 and propose methods for unbiased VidSGG\footnote{To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to investigate biases in the SGA task and assess the robustness of both VidSGG and SGA models.. .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Similarly, TEMPURA \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023 addresses biases in relationship prediction with memory-guided training to generate balanced relationship representations and applies a Gaussian Mixture Model to reduce predictive uncertainty.. Long-Tail Learning.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[scale=0.3]{paper_sec/images/OverviewUpdated.pdf} \caption{Overview of \methodname \textbf{(a) Pipeline.} The forward pass of \methodname begins with an ORPU, where initial object proposals are generated for each observed frame. These object representations are then fed to STPUs designed to construct spatio-temporal context-aware relationship representations of interacting objects. \methodname applied to both tasks VidSGG and SGA remains mostly the same, with an additional LDPU unit added for SGA to anticipate relationship representations for future frames. These observed (for VidSGG)/ anticipated (for SGA) relationship representations are then decoded to construct STSGs. \textbf{(b) Conventional Training.} Previous approaches estimated loss for all relationship predicates (head and tail classes). \textbf{(c) Masked Training.} With the inherent long-tailed nature of the STSG datasets, conventional training results in biased VidSGG and SGA models. Thus, to de-bias the training and learn an unbiased model, in \methodname, instead of estimating loss for all relationship predicates, we estimate a masked loss, where we selectively mask the labels corresponding to dominant head classes and void their contribution in learning. \textbf{(d) Curriculum-Guided Mask Generation.} In \methodname, we introduce a curriculum-based approach for masking relationship predicate labels during training. At each iteration, we adjust the selection of masked predicates to balance the class distribution progressively. As illustrated, initially, the model trains on the original, long-tailed distribution. As training advances, we systematically mask predicate labels from the head classes, gradually shifting the distribution toward uniformity. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-173: Consider a video segment \( V_{1 ^{T \) from a video \( V \), modeled as a sequence of frames at discrete time steps \( t = 1, 2, \ldots, T \): $V_{1 ^{T = \{ I^{1 , I^{2 , \ldots, I^{T \}$ where \( T \) denotes the total number of frames in the segment.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Each object \( o_{k ^{t \) is characterized by: A bounding box \( \mathbf{b _{k ^{t \in [0,1]^{4 \), representing its spatial location and A category label \( \mathbf{c _{k ^{t \in \mathcal{C \), where \( \mathcal{C \) is the set of object categories.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: A relationship instance is then defined as the triplet: $r_{ijk ^{t = (o_{i ^{t , p_{ijk ^{t , o_{j ^{t )$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Thus, the scene graph \( \mathcal{G ^{t \) for frame \( t \) consists of all such relationship triplets: $\mathcal{G ^{t = \{ r_{ijk ^{t \}_{i,j,k}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: These distributions are normalized, satisfying: $ \sum_{k \hat{c _{ik ^{t = 1, \quad \sum_{k \hat{p _{ijk ^{t = 1.$ We distinguish and clarify the objectives of Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) as follows: (a) VidSGG.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: Here, \( H \) denotes the anticipation horizon\footnote{Following the definition and assumptions from SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , we assume the continuity of observed objects in future frames.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Following the literature in Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG) and Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA), we implement three distinct strategies for constructing scene graphs: (a) With Constraint Strategy: This strategy imposes a unique interaction constraint between every pair of objects within a scene.
elements.sent-2411-181: Thus, it can be easily extended to de-bias any VidSGG/SGA method\footnote{Note: \methodname does not train models for both tasks simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Instead, it adapts existing approaches for VidSGG/SGA separately and learns the corresponding VidSGG/SGA unbiased estimators.
elements.sent-2411-183: These are the classes that are over-represented in the dataset and often lead to biased predictions.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our approach aims to reduce their influence while focusing on under-represented tail relationships.
elements.sent-2411-185: Furthermore, our curriculum-driven mask generation method enables the model to iteratively refine its bias mitigation strategy over time, resulting in a balanced performance.

elements.sent-2411-187: Pipeline Given that our approach builds upon existing object-centric VidSGG/SGA methods—most of which share similar representation processing pipelines—we detail the essential architectural components necessary for our framework to effectively adapt a method and learn an unbiased estimator\footnote{Current methods in the literature typically incorporate at least one additional component beyond those specified here.}
elements.sent-2411-188: It then employs a transformer encoder \cite{vaswani_et_al_2017 to integrate temporal information and generate temporally consistent object representations (see Eq.~\ref{eq:object_representations ): \begin{equation}\mathbf{V}_{i}^{(n)} = \operatorname{ObjectEncoder}\left(\mathbb{Q} = \mathbb{K} = \mathbb{V} = \mathbf{V}_{i}^{(n-1)} \right) \label{eq:object_representations}\end{equation} (\RNum{2) Spatio-Temporal Context Processing Unit (STPU): learns spatio-temporal context-aware relationship representations, it first constructs representations for interacting objects, then refines spatial context with an encoder, and finally use a transformer encoder to integrate spatio-temporal context information.}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Specifically, let the relationship representation for each observed frame $I^{t}$ as $\mathbf{z _{ij ^t$, where $\mathbf{Z ^{t}$ is formed by stacking all relationship features $\{\mathbf{z _{ij ^{t}\}_{ij}$ corresponding to the objects within the frame.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Next, it constructs the matrix $\mathbf{Z _{ij}$ by stacking the relationship representations $\topsequence{\mathbf{z _{ij {t {1 {T}$ over all observed frames.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: This matrix $\mathbf{Z _{ij}$ is then passed through a temporal encoder, which aggregates temporal information across the representations (refer LDPU \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 for SGA task): \begin{equation}{[Z^{t}}]^{(n)} = \operatorname{SpatialEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = [{Z^{t}}]^{(n-1)} \right) Z_{ij}^{(n)} = \operatorname{TemporalEncoder}\left(Q = K = V = Z_{ij}^{(n)} \right)\end{equation} (\RNum{3) Relationship Predicate Decoders: Current VidSGG methods utilize a two-layer MLPs to decode the output relationship representations from STPU described as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:PredicateClassificationVidSGG} \hat{\mathbf{p}}^t_{ij} = \operatorname{PredClassifier}\left(\mathbf{z}^t_{ij} \right), \forall t \in [1, \bar{T}]\end{equation} We note that for SGA task, following SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 , we employ two relationship predicate decoders.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: One for the observed relationship representations and the other for the anticipated representations described as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:PredicateClassificationSGA} \hat{p}^t_{ij} = \operatorname{PredClassifier}_{observed}\left(z^t_{ij} \right), \forall t \in [1, T] \hat{p}^t_{ij} = \operatorname{PredClassifier}_{anticipated}\left(z^t_{ij} \right), \forall t \in [T+1, \bar{T}]\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our approach stems from the observation that instead of re-weighting training data points which, in our case, entails uniformly adjusting the loss contributed by all relationship predicates derived from observed object pairs across all frames of video), selectively masking the loss contributed by head relationship classes helps learn unbiased estimators.
elements.sent-2411-194: During training, for epoch $e$ and mask-ratio $\mathscr{R _{m}$, Mask Generator generates a set of relationship predicate label masks $\mathcal{M ^{(e) =\{\{\mathbf{m ^t_{ijk}\}_{ijk}\}_{t}$ where $\mathbf{m ^t_{ijk \in \{0, 1\}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Let $\mathcal{L _{p^t_{ijk }$ represent any loss defined over a relationship predicate\footnote{(a) Two interacting objects (i,j) at time (t) can have multiple relationship predicates $\{p^t_{ijk}\}_{k}$ that describe the nature of the interaction; (b) We re-use the predicate loss $\mathcal{L _{p^t_{ijk }$ from the method employed for adaptation.. .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: A mask initialized i.e. setting $\mathbf{m ^t_{ijk =1$ voids the contribution of loss $\mathcal{L _{p^t_{ijk }$ and results in masked loss $\mathscr{L _{p^t_{ij }$ given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RelMaskedLoss} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} = (1-\mathbf{m}^t_{ijk}) * \mathcal{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} = \begin{cases} \mathcal{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} \text{if } \mathbf{m}^t_{ijk} = 0 0 \text{if } \mathbf{m}^t_{ijk} = 1 \end{cases}\end{equation} VidSGG.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-197: Thus, the objective when \methodname is employed to SceneSayer \cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 includes (1) Object Classification Loss, (2) Masked Predicate Classification Loss over observed relationship representations, (3) Masked Predicate Classification Loss over anticipated relationship representations, (4) Bounding Box Regression Loss and (5) Reconstruction Loss as shown in Eq~\ref{eq:SGAMaskedLoss . \begin{equation}\label{eq:SGAMaskedLoss} \underbrace{ L_{i} = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} L_{i}^{t} }_(1); \quad \underbrace{ \mathscr{L}_{gen} = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} \mathscr{L}_{gen}^t, \mathscr{L}_{gen}^t = \sum_{ijk} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} }_Masked Observed Predicate Classification Loss (2) \underbrace{ \mathscr{L}_{ant}^{(1:T)} = \sum_{t=T+1}^{\min(T+H, \bar{T})} \mathscr{L}_{ant}^t, \quad \mathscr{L}_{ant}^t = \sum_{ijk} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} }_Masked Anticipated Predicate Classification Loss (3) L = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} \left(\lambda_{1} \mathscr{L}_{gen}^{t} + \lambda_{2} \sum_{i} L_{i}^{t} \right) + \sum_{T=3}^{\bar{T}-1} \left( \lambda_{3} \mathscr{L}_{ant}^{(1:T)} + \lambda_{4} L_{boxes}^{(1:T)} + \lambda_{5} L_{recon}^{(1:T)} \right)\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\label{eq:SGAMaskedLoss} \underbrace{ L_{i} = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} L_{i}^{t} }_(1); \quad \underbrace{ \mathscr{L}_{gen} = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} \mathscr{L}_{gen}^t, \mathscr{L}_{gen}^t = \sum_{ijk} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} }_Masked Observed Predicate Classification Loss (2) \underbrace{ \mathscr{L}_{ant}^{(1:T)} = \sum_{t=T+1}^{\min(T+H, \bar{T})} \mathscr{L}_{ant}^t, \quad \mathscr{L}_{ant}^t = \sum_{ijk} \mathscr{L}_{p^t_{ijk}} }_Masked Anticipated Predicate Classification Loss (3) L = \sum_{t=1}^{\bar{T}} \left(\lambda_{1} \mathscr{L}_{gen}^{t} + \lambda_{2} \sum_{i} L_{i}^{t} \right) + \sum_{T=3}^{\bar{T}-1} \left( \lambda_{3} \mathscr{L}_{ant}^{(1:T)} + \lambda_{4} L_{boxes}^{(1:T)} + \lambda_{5} L_{recon}^{(1:T)} \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-198: We observed that by tuning the mask-ratio ($\mathscr{R _{m}$) (i.e., the fraction of annotations masked), \methodname can learn estimators along a spectrum, from those exhibiting bias to those that are unbiased.}
elements.sent-2411-199: We propose curriculum-guided mask generation to enable models to progressively refine their bias mitigation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-200: Thus, \methodname employs a simple yet effective curriculum learning method to generate masks for relationship predicate labels to train VidSGG/SGA models.
elements.sent-2411-201: Simply put, as training progresses, we stochastically mask the loss of head classes (easier examples), allowing the model to focus on learning about tail classes (harder examples) and the number of masks increases each epoch.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \SetCustomAlgoRuledWidth{\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon { \KwIn{Epoch: $e$, Sampling Ratio: $\mathscr{R}_{s}$, Dataset Annotations: $\mathcal{D}$, Total predicate labels: $N$, Total predicates: $\mathcal{P}$, Videos: $\mathscr{V}$ } \KwOut{Masks: $\mathcal{M}^{(e)}$} \textcolor{blue}{*** Determine Target Counts [can also be a fixed input] ***} $\mathscr{R}_{m} = e \times \mathscr{R}_{s}$ \quad \textcolor{blue}{** Masking Ratio **} \; $N_{\text{target}} = \operatorname{round}(N \times \mathscr{R}_{m})$\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Curriculum-based sampling probabilities $Prob[rel]$ ***}\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Equally weighted distribution [can also be learnt] *** }\; Set $Prob[rel] = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}|}$ \; Sample target counts $Tar[rel]$ from Multinomial distribution: $Tar[rel] \sim \operatorname{Multinomial}(N_{\text{target}}, Prob[rel])$ \; \textcolor{blue}{*** Randomly sample instances of relationships in the dataset based on the target counts and construct filtered dataset ***} \; Construct filtered dataset $\mathcal{F}$ \quad \textcolor{blue}{** See Appendix for details**} \; \textcolor{blue}{*** Construct masks such that any relation present in the filtered dataset is unmasked, while all others are masked. ***} \; Initialize empty mask list $\mathcal{M}^{(e)}$\; \ForEach{Video $v$ in $\mathscr{V}$}{ Initialize video mask $\mathcal{M}_v$\; \ForEach{Frame $f$ in $v$}{ Initialize frame mask $\mathcal{M}_f$\; \ForEach{Object $o$ in $f$}{ Initialize object mask $\mathcal{M}_o$\; Original relations $\mathcal{R}_{o}$ from $\mathcal{D}[v][f]$\; \ForEach{Relation $rel$ in $\mathcal{R}_{o}$}{ \eIf{$rel \in \mathcal{F}[v][f]$}{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 0$\; }{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 1$\; } } Add $\mathcal{M}_o$ to $\mathcal{M}_f$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_f$ to $\mathcal{M}_v$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_v$ to $\mathcal{M}^{(e)}$\; } } \caption{Mask Generation} \label{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{paper_sec/images/CorruptionsUpdated.pdf} \caption{\textbf{(a) Robustness Evaluation Pipeline:} We present a methodology to assess the robustness of trained VidSGG and SGA models when faced with input distribution shifts. Specifically, we systematically introduce corruptions to the frames of test videos, which are then used as inputs for the trained models. \textbf{(b) Corrupted Frames:} We illustrate the frames obtained by inducing various categories of corruptions (see Appendix Sec.4 for details).} \label{fig:input_corruptions}

elements.sent-2411-206: Although \methodname is designed to learn estimators ranging from biased to unbiased through tuning the mask ratio in mask generation algorithm Alg~\ref{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation , our experiments on ActionGenome revealed that a fixed Stochastic Masking Ratio of 0.9 delivered similar performance in mean recall metrics to a sequential strategy that started at a masking ratio of 0.6 and gradually increased to 0.9.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Moreover, the fixed ratio also showed competitive results within fewer training epochs.
elements.sent-2411-208: Therefore, in the paper to report results, we opted for a fixed Masking Ratio of 0.9\footnote{Due to the skewed distribution of the dataset, using a 0.9 masking ratio creates a scenario where, in each training epoch, the model primarily samples head (easier) classes while leaving tail (harder) classes unaltered.. .}
elements.sent-2411-209: For completeness, we provide a thorough ablation in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-210: We first trained and evaluated the chosen baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-211: Next, we integrated \methodnamenosp into these baselines and conducted retraining and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-212: Our experiments encompassed three VidSGG modes SGDET, SGCLS, and PREDCLS and each included three distinct settings: (1) With Constraint, (2) No Constraint, and (3) Semi Constraint.
elements.sent-2411-213: As demonstrated in Table~\ref{tab:sgg_combined_recall_results , models trained using \methodname consistently outperformed the chosen VidSGG baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Moreover, our results are on par and occasionally surpass SOTA unbiased learning methods \cite{nag_et_al_tempura_2023}\footnote{Note: (a) SOTA at the time of writing this paper, (b) These improvements were achieved without introducing any new architectural components .}

elements.sent-2411-215: We employed a procedure for training and evaluating SGA models that closely mirrors our approach for VidSGG.
elements.sent-2411-216: First, we trained and evaluated the chosen baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-217: We then integrated these models into the \methodname framework, retrained them, and re-evaluated their performance.
elements.sent-2411-218: Insights: (1) The results demonstrate an improvement of $\sim$12\% in mR@10 metric across all modes in STTran++ and DSGDetr++.
elements.sent-2411-219: Although the performance is sometimes inferior, it closely aligns with the non-\methodname variants.
elements.sent-2411-220: In contrast, the \methodname-trained model dropped from 65.5 to 57.5.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{paper_sec/images/RecallComparison.pdf} \caption{Predicate Classification recall performance. of models trained using existing VidSGG methods STTran, DSGDetr, and models trained using their \methodname adaptations. In each row, we compare the R@10/50 performance of each relationship category without corruptions(left) and with corruptions(right) in input data).} \label{fig:RecallComparison}
elements.sent-2411-221: Insights: (1) We noticed that the drop in performance of both VidSGG and SGA models trained using \methodname is lower than the models trained with traditional approaches (see Tables~\ref{tab:sgg_corruptions_results , \ref{tab:sga_corruptions_results ).}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Thus, \methodname trained models focus on a broader spatio-temporal context and other discovered associations to infer missing connections when frames are corrupted.
elements.sent-2411-223: This regularizing effect (similar in spirit to dropout) pushes the model to rely on shared patterns across categories, not just the apparent cues in dominant categories.
elements.sent-2411-224: Consequently, when inputs are noisy, the model is more likely to recover the underlying spatio-temporal relationships using generalized features.
elements.sent-2411-225: We introduced \methodnamenosp, a framework that employs masked training with partial gradients to shift the focus of learning toward tail classes without sacrificing performance on head classes.
elements.sent-2411-226: To further evaluate the practical applicability of \methodname, we proposed two new tasks: Robust VidSGG and Robust SGA.
elements.sent-2411-227: Future work includes developing robust learning techniques \cite{ilyas2019adversarialexamplesbugsfeatures, peddi_et_al_uai_2022, carlini2019evaluatingadversarialrobustness for both VidSGG and SGA tasks and exploring the application of unbiased VidSGG and SGA in long-tailed regimes such as error recognition \cite{peddi2024captaincook4ddatasetunderstandingerrors, sener2022assembly101largescalemultiviewvideo , action anticipation \cite{nawhal2022rethinkinglearningapproacheslongterm , grasping \cite{xiang2024grasping etc..}}}}

elements.sent-2411-235: Long-Tailed Distributions: Real-world visual relationships are dominated by a few frequent head classes, while many rare but critical tail classes are underrepresented.
elements.sent-2411-236: This imbalance leads to biased models that fail to generalize effectively across all relationship types, compromising nuanced and accurate scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-237: Robustness to Distribution Shifts: Models often struggle with real-world factors such as lighting variations, occlusions, or environmental changes.
elements.sent-2411-238: These distributional shifts degrade performance, limiting the applicability of scene graph models in dynamic and unpredictable environments.
elements.sent-2411-239: Improving Unbiasedness in Scene Understanding: A long-tailed distribution skews model learning towards frequent classes, leading to biased predictions.
elements.sent-2411-240: Correctly predicting rare relationships is vital for tasks such as autonomous driving, human-robot interaction, and security surveillance, where underrepresented classes can carry critical contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-241: Enhancing Real-World Applicability: Distributional shifts are unavoidable in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-242: Ensuring robustness allows STSG models to remain reliable under practical deployment conditions, bolstering trust and usability.
elements.sent-2411-243: Thus, concisely, we re-iterate the contributions of the proposed work:.
elements.sent-2411-244: Unbiased Learning with Curriculum-Guided Masking: The proposed \methodname framework leverages curriculum learning and loss masking to prioritize tail classes progressively during training.
elements.sent-2411-245: Introduction of Robustness Metrics: Two new tasks—Robust Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Generation and Robust Scene Graph Anticipation—evaluate model resilience to input corruptions (a step towards analyzing the performance of STSG models under realistic conditions).
elements.sent-2411-246: Learning to represent static visual data like 2D and 3D images as spatial graphs, with objects as nodes and relationships as edges, is called Image Scene Graph Generation (ImgSGG).
elements.sent-2411-247: This field gained momentum with the foundational Visual Genome project~\cite{krishna_et_al_visual_2017 , advancing 2D ImgSGG research.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Building on this foundation, \cite{kim_et_al_3dsgg_2019 expanded the task to encompass static 3D scene data, including RGB and depth information.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Object interactions over time provide richer context when dealing with dynamic visual content like videos.
elements.sent-2411-250: Converting such content into structured Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs (STSGs), where nodes represent objects and edges capture their temporal relationships, is called Video Scene Graph Generation (VidSGG).
elements.sent-2411-251: The research community has concentrated on improving representation learning through sophisticated object-centric architectures like STTran \cite{cong_et_al_sttran_2021 and RelFormer \cite{shit_et_al_relformer_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: These include Open-Vocabulary ImgSGG \cite{chen_et_al_expanding_scene_graph_2023 , which expands the range of recognizable objects and relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Weakly Supervised ImgSGG \cite{kim_et_al_llm4sgg_2023 to reduce the dependency on extensive labelled data by leveraging weak supervision techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-254: Panoptic ImgSGG \cite{zhou_et_al_vlprompt_2023 where panoptic segmentation has been integrated to enhance scene graph representations.}
elements.sent-2411-255: Zero-Shot ImgSGG \cite{zhao_et_al_less_2023, li2023zeroshotvisualrelationdetection to enable the detection of unseen visual relationships without explicit labels.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Shifting gears from identification and generating scene graphs, recently, Peddi et al.\cite{peddi_et_al_scene_sayer_2024 introduced the Scene Graph Anticipation (SGA) task to anticipate STSGs for future frames.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Alongside these developments, foundation models have advanced various ImgSGG task variants \cite{chen_et_al_expanding_scene_graph_2023, kim_et_al_llm4sgg_2023, zhou_et_al_vlprompt_2023, zhao_et_al_less_2023, li2023zeroshotvisualrelationdetection .}
elements.sent-2411-258: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to systematically investigate model biases in the SGA task and assess the robustness of both VidSGG and SGA models.
elements.sent-2411-259: In contrast to the above methods, although \methodname shares the goal of training unbiased VidSGG models, it does so without additional architectural components.
elements.sent-2411-260: By modifying the training procedure, \methodname achieves comparable performance and occasionally exceeds the results of these existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-261: Early approaches involved re-weighting samples inversely proportional to class frequency \cite{huang_et_al_2016, wang_et_al_tail_2017 , but this often led to suboptimal performance on real-world data \cite{cui_et_al_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: To improve upon this, advanced methods like label-distribution-aware margin loss with Deferred Re-Weighting (DRW) were proposed \cite{cao_et_al_label_dist_margin_nips_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: Equalization Loss (EQL) \cite{tan_et_al_eql_2020 showed that ignoring discouraging gradients for tail classes can prevent adverse effects on model learning.}
elements.sent-2411-264: The Class-Balanced (CB) loss \cite{cui_et_al_2019 re-weights the loss based on the effective number of samples per class, achieving notable performance in single-label classification.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Asymmetric Loss (ASL) \cite{ridnik_et_al_asl_loss_2020 and Distribution-Balanced (DB) loss \cite{wu_et_al_distribution_balanced_2020 focus on balancing positive and negative labels in multi-label classification.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: Other approaches include transferring knowledge from head to tail classes \cite{ouyang_et_al_factors_finetuning_2016, wang_et_al_tail_2017 and designing better training objectives through metric learning \cite{you_et_al_exemplar_clustering_2018, zhang_et_al_range_loss_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-267: LFME \cite{Xiang2020LearningFM merges multiple experts using self-paced knowledge distillation, while RIDE employs diversity loss and dynamic routing for sample assignment.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Techniques like SMOTE \cite{chawla_et_al_smote_2002 create synthetic samples for minority classes, while under-sampling methods reduce samples from majority classes \cite{drummond_et_al_c45_kdd_2003 .}}
elements.sent-2411-269: Approaches, such as concatenating frames from different video clips \cite{zhang_et_al_2021 , offer a different way to balance data, particularly spatio-temporal data.}
elements.sent-2411-270: FrameStack \cite{zhang_et_al_2021 performs frame-level sampling guided by running average precision to improve tail class representation without explicitly differentiating classes at the feature level.}
elements.sent-2411-271: The Bilateral-Branch Network (BBN) \cite{zhou_et_al_bbn_2019 uses cumulative learning to balance representation learning and classifier discrimination.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Kang et al. \cite{kang_et_al_iclr_2019 demonstrated that decoupling representation learning from classifier training prevents head classes from overshadowing tail classes.}
elements.sent-2411-273: While Kang et al. \cite{kang_et_al_iclr_2019 argued that resampling might not always be necessary if classifier training is focused correctly, Zhou et al. \cite{zhou_et_al_bbn_2019 showed that standard resampling could harm representation learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-274: Li et al. \cite{li_et_al_gaussian_clouded_cvpr_2022 proposed Gaussian Clouded Logit Adjustment to perturb class logits, adjusting decision boundaries for better generalization across classes.}
elements.sent-2411-275: This approach aims to enhance learning efficiency by aligning the difficulty of training data with the model's learning capacity at each stage \footnote{It's important to differentiate CL from other dynamic sampling techniques such as self-paced learning \cite{kumar_et_al_self_paced_2010 , boosting \cite{freund_boosting_1997 , hard example mining \cite{shrivastava_et_al_hard_example_mining_2016 , and active learning \cite{schein_et_al_al_2005 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: While these methods also adjust the training data based on certain criteria, they typically rely on the model's current performance or hypotheses to select samples rather than following a predefined difficulty progression as in CL.}\cite{kumar_et_al_self_paced_2010, graves_et_al_auto_cr_2017, hacohen_et_al_cl_tdn_2019, han_et_al_dyn_een_2022 .
elements.sent-2411-277: A primary obstacle is distinguishing between easy and hard training samples.
elements.sent-2411-278: The starting small concept influences our methodology \cite{elman_starting_small_1993 , which recommends initiating learning with easier tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Unlike conventional Curriculum Learning (CL) methods that introduce data progressively, our approach utilizes the entire training dataset from the start.
elements.sent-2411-280: We adopt label selection to mask the loss function, offering a unique strategy that impacts the learning process while keeping all training examples in play.
elements.sent-2411-281: This approach not only streamlines the implementation of CL but also tackles the difficulties of determining and scheduling the complexity of training data.
elements.sent-2411-282: Limited Scope of Datasets: Experiments are primarily conducted on the Action Genome dataset.. \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-283: This is a primary concern of the field as the Action Genome is the only large-scale dataset available as a testbed for the Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph tasks.. \end{itemize.}

elements.sent-2411-293: Here, we present the complete algorithm for the proposed unbiased learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-294: Our framework has four key components.
elements.sent-2411-295: Note: ORPU, STPU, and the predicate decoders can be adapted from any VidSGG or SGA method following an object-centric framework.
elements.sent-2411-296: Focusing on underrepresented classes helps the model achieve a balanced class distribution.
elements.sent-2411-297: This has two components as explained in the main paper: (a) Curriculum-Guided Mask Generation and (b) Masked Predicate Classification Loss.
elements.sent-2411-298: First, we provide the complete algorithm for Mask Generation and give an overview of the loss function employed.
elements.sent-2411-299: In the subsequent sections, we clearly explain and contrast the loss functions for the original and the proposed \methodname variants.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \DontPrintSemicolon { \KwIn{Epoch: $e$, Sampling Ratio: $\mathscr{R}_{s}$, Dataset Annotations: $\mathcal{D}$, Total predicate labels: $N$, Total predicates: $\mathcal{P}$, Videos: $\mathscr{V}$} \KwOut{Filtered Dataset: $\mathcal{F}$} \textcolor{blue}{*** Determine Target Counts [can also be a fixed input] ***} $\mathscr{R}_{m} = e \times \mathscr{R}_{s}$ \quad \textcolor{blue}{** Masking Ratio **} \; $N_{\text{target}} = \operatorname{round}(N \times \mathscr{R}_{m})$\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Curriculum-based sampling probabilities $Prob[rel]$ ***}\; \textcolor{blue}{*** Equally weighted distribution [can also be learnt] *** }\; Set $Prob[rel] = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}|}$ \; Sample target counts $Tar[rel]$ from Multinomial distribution: $Tar[rel] \sim \operatorname{Multinomial}(N_{\text{target}}, Prob[rel])$ \; \textcolor{blue}{*** Randomly sample instances of relationships in the dataset based on the target counts and construct filtered dataset ***} \; Initialize positions $\mathscr{P}[rel]$ to collect occurrences of $rel$\; \ForEach{$v$, $f$ in $\mathcal{D}$}{ \ForEach{Relation $rel$ in $\mathcal{D}[v][f]$}{ Append position $(v, f, \text{index})$ to $\mathscr{P}[rel]$\; } } Initialize empty set $\mathcal{K}$\; \textcolor{blue}{*** These relations are ignored and are omitted from the filtered dataset constructed below ***} \; \ForEach{Relation $rel$}{ Randomly select $Tar[rel]$ positions which should be masked from $\mathscr{P}[rel]$ and add the remaining to $\mathcal{K}$\; } \textcolor{blue}{*** Filter Data ***} \; Initialize filtered data $\mathcal{F}$\; \ForEach{$v$, $f$ in $\mathcal{D}$}{ Initialize filtered frame $\mathcal{F}[v][f]$\; \ForEach{Relation at position $(v, f, i)$}{ \If{$(v, f, i) \in \mathcal{K}$}{ Add relation to $\mathcal{F}[v][f]$\; } } } } \caption{Filtered Dataset Construction} \label{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \DontPrintSemicolon { \KwIn{Epoch: $e$, Dataset Annotations: $\mathcal{D}$, Videos: $\mathscr{V}$, Filtered Dataset: $\mathcal{F}$} \KwOut{Masks: $\mathcal{M}$} Initialize empty mask list $\mathcal{M}$\; \ForEach{Video $v$ in $\mathscr{V}$}{ Initialize video mask $\mathcal{M}_v$\; \ForEach{Frame $f$ in $v$}{ Initialize frame mask $\mathcal{M}_f$\; \ForEach{Object $o$ in $f$}{ Initialize object mask $\mathcal{M}_o$\; Original relations $\mathcal{R}_{o}$ from $\mathcal{D}[v][f]$\; \ForEach{Relation $rel$ in $\mathcal{R}_{o}$}{ \eIf{$rel \in \mathcal{F}[v][f]$}{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 0$\; }{ Set mask value $\mathcal{M}_o[rel] = 1$\; } } Add $\mathcal{M}_o$ to $\mathcal{M}_f$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_f$ to $\mathcal{M}_v$\; } Add $\mathcal{M}_v$ to $\mathcal{M}^{(e)}$\; } } \caption{Mask Generation} \label{alg:cur_guided_mask_generation}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sup_sec/images/ImparTail_STTran.pdf} \caption{(a) Architectural Components. In STTran, the Object Representations Processing Unit (ORPU) primarily consists of an object detector and the visual features output by the object detector. Then, the Spatio-Temporal Context Processing Unit (STPU )takes these visual features as input and first constructs relationship representations utilizing the features of interacting objects; then, these relationship representations are fed to a spatial encoder and a transformer encoder. Thus, the spatio-temporal context-aware representations output by the temporal encoder are fed into the predicate classifier for final predictions. Finally, these representations are decoded for predicate classification. (b) Loss Function. The primary difference between \colorbox{\baselinecolor}{STTran} loss and the proposed \colorbox{\methodcolor}{\methodname + STTran} loss is illustrated using highlighting the employed losses. We do not mask any predicate label in \colorbox{\baselinecolor}{STTran} loss. In contrast, in the proposed \colorbox{\methodcolor}{\methodname + STTran} loss, we mask the losses corresponding to the head classes as generated by following the curriculum-based strategy.} \label{fig:sup_sttran}
elements.sent-2411-300: Here, $\mathcal{L _{p^t_{ij }$ represents multi-label margin loss and is computed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:predicate_classification_loss} \mathcal{L}_{p^t_{ij}}=\sum_{u \in \mathcal{P}^{+}} \sum_{v \in \mathcal{P}^{-}} \max (0,1-\hat{\mathbf{p}}^t_{ij}[v]+\hat{\mathbf{p}}^t_{ij}[u])\end{equation}.}}
